questions.topic: How do I start my thesis process at Karelia UAS?
questions.correct_answer: Start by choosing a topic based on your professional interest that relates to your specialisation field and its development perspectives. Discuss the topic with the person responsible for thesis processes in your programme. Once the topic is accepted, log in to Wihi at https://karelia.wihi.fi and submit your topic proposal. After approval in Wihi, an advisor is assigned and you can begin your literature review. Please refer to the official thesis instructions for more detailed guidance: https://libguides.karelia.fi/thesis_instructions

llm_answers:
To start your thesis process at Karelia University of Applied Sciences (Karelia UAS), follow the steps outlined in the official Karelia UAS thesis instructions:

1.  **Choose a topic**: Select a topic that interests you, arises from a practical need, and relates to your specialisation field and its development perspectives.
2.  **Discuss with your supervisor**: Discuss your chosen topic with the person responsible for the thesis processes in your programme.
3.  **Create a topic proposal in Wihi**: Log in to Wihi at https://karelia.wihi.fi using your Karelia user ID (username and password), then create a topic proposal. For pair work, only one student makes the topic proposal and adds the other student in the *Invite group members* field.
4.  **Submit the topic proposal**: Write a working title for your thesis, enter the start date and estimated end date, choose the thesis type (Product-based / Research / Portfolio-Diary / Master's thesis), describe the content, objectives, and outcomes of your thesis, and add commissioner information if applicable.

Once you have submitted your topic proposal in Wihi, it will be reviewed by your supervisor. If approved, an advisor will be assigned to guide you through the thesis process.

Please refer to the official Karelia UAS thesis instructions at: https://libguides.karelia.fi/thesis_instructions for more detailed guidance on starting your thesis process.

---

**NB!** The commission agreement is not included as an appendix to the thesis report.

**Collaborative Thesis Work**

Yes, you can work on a thesis with your friend. The process is similar to individual work, but there are some additional steps and considerations.

**Approval of Collaborative Thesis Topic**

When you have found a topic, discuss it with the person responsible for the thesis processes in your programme. It's recommended that collaborative theses made with industry and business life are also supervised by a representative of the commissioning organisation.

**Collaborative Commission Agreement**

If the thesis is commissioned, the student(s), the commissioning organisation, and the university of applied sciences sign a written agreement. The agreement determines the obligations and responsibilities of each party and should include a clause on immaterial rights whenever applicable.

**Wihi System for Collaborative Work**

Use the Wihi system to manage your collaborative work. Both students must log in to Wihi at **https://karelia.wihi.fi**, create a topic proposal, and add each other as group members. The working title, start date, estimated end date, thesis type, content, objectives, and outcomes should be entered together.

**Collaborative Thesis Phases**

When the topic proposal is approved, a Thesis phases window appears in Wihi. Plan and describe tasks for the collaborative project using *Create Task*. Both students can see all files and messages shared between them in Wihi, except confidential files which are only visible to both students.

**Collaborative Data Management**

Plan data management together with your friend. This includes managing research material, such as measurement results, surveys, interviews, audio and video recordings, research diaries and notes, drawings, photographs, text samples, and other collected data.

> For more guidance, visit the official Karelia UAS thesis instructions at:
> https://libguides.karelia.fi/thesis_instructions

A literature review is a crucial part of the thesis process at Karelia UAS. I'll provide an explanation based on the official instructions.

**What is a Literature Review?**

A literature review is a written document that sets the premises and defines the knowledge base for your thesis. It's a critical analysis of existing research, theories, and concepts related to your topic. The purpose of a literature review is to:

1. **Identify key themes and concepts**: Understand the main ideas, theories, and models relevant to your topic.
2. **Synthesize existing knowledge**: Combine and analyze the findings from various studies, articles, and sources to create a comprehensive understanding of the subject.
3. **Highlight gaps in current research**: Identify areas where further investigation is needed or where your thesis can contribute new insights.

**What should I include in my Literature Review?**

When writing your literature review, focus on:

1. **Relevant sources**: Use academic articles, books, and other credible sources that are directly related to your topic.
2. **Key themes and concepts**: Discuss the main ideas, theories, and models that are relevant to your research question or problem statement.
3. **Critical analysis**: Evaluate the strengths and limitations of each source, highlighting areas where they contribute to or challenge existing knowledge.
4. **Synthesis**: Combine the findings from multiple sources to create a comprehensive understanding of the subject.

**How long should my Literature Review be?**

The literature review should be approximately 10 pages in length. This will give you enough space to critically analyze and synthesize the existing research without overwhelming your reader with too much information.

**What are the key characteristics of a good Literature Review?**

A well-written literature review should:

1. **Be clear and concise**: Use simple language to explain complex concepts.
2. **Be logically organized**: Use headings, subheadings, and paragraphs to structure your review.
3. **Include in-text citations**: Properly cite all sources used in the review.
4. **Have a balanced tone**: Critically evaluate each source while maintaining an objective tone.

Please refer to the official Karelia UAS thesis instructions for more detailed guidance on writing a literature review: https://libguides.karelia.fi/thesis_instructions

A literature review (Lit Review) is a crucial part of the thesis process at Karelia UAS. It's a written document that sets the premises and defines the knowledge base for your thesis.

**When to do it:**

You should start writing the Lit Review after your thesis topic has been approved by your supervisor. This typically happens during the initial stages of your thesis process, before you begin drafting your thesis plan.

**What is its purpose:**

The Lit Review serves several purposes:

1.  **Establishes the knowledge base**: It provides an overview of the existing research and literature related to your topic.
2.  **Sets the premises**: It outlines the theoretical or professional framework that underpins your thesis.
3.  **Defines the scope**: It helps you identify the key themes, concepts, and areas of focus for your thesis.

**What should it include:**

A typical Lit Review should cover the following elements:

1.  **Introduction**: A brief overview of the topic and its significance.
2.  **Background**: An explanation of the context and history surrounding the topic.
3.  **Theoretical framework**: An examination of the theoretical concepts and models relevant to your research question or problem.
4.  **Literature review**: A critical analysis of existing research and literature related to your topic.
5.  **Conclusion**: A summary of the key findings and implications for your thesis.

**How long should it be:**

The Lit Review is typically around 10 pages in length, but this may vary depending on the specific requirements of your degree programme.

**What format should I use?**

Use the Karelia UAS thesis template (.dotx) to ensure correct formatting throughout. Save working drafts in .docx format.

For more guidance, visit the official Karelia UAS thesis instructions at: https://libguides.karelia.fi/thesis_instructions

Remember to use credible sources, such as academic journals, books, and government reports, and cite them correctly using the Karelia UAS referencing style.

The Karelia UAS thesis instructions specify a particular layout and formatting style for the thesis report. Here's an example of what the correct layout might look like:

**Cover Page**

* Title: centered, font size 14, bold
* Student name: below title, font size 12
* Degree programme: below student name, font size 10
* University logo: top right corner

Example:
```
Karelia UAS Thesis Template

Turunen Tanja
Bachelor of Engineering in Information Technology
Karelia University of Applied Sciences
```

**Abstract**

* Font size 12, justified alignment
* Approximately 150-200 words, divided into paragraphs separated by blank lines
* Keywords: 3-4 keywords from the General Finnish Ontology (YSO)

Example:
```
Abstract

This thesis explores the impact of artificial intelligence on customer service in e-commerce. The study aims to identify the benefits and challenges of implementing AI-powered chatbots in online stores.

The literature review reveals that AI can improve customer satisfaction, reduce response times, and increase sales. However, it also raises concerns about data privacy, security, and job displacement.

The research methodology involves a mixed-methods approach, combining both qualitative and quantitative data collection methods.

The results show that AI-powered chatbots can significantly improve customer service in e-commerce, but also highlight the need for careful implementation and monitoring.
```

**Contents**

* Font size 14, bold
* Placed after the title page and abstract
* Headings must appear exactly as in the text

Example:
```
Contents

1. Introduction ....................................................... 3
2. Literature Review ................................................. 5
3. Methodology ........................................................ 10
4. Results ................................................................ 15
5. Discussion ................................................................... 20
6. Conclusion .................................................................... 25
7. References ..................................................................... 30
8. Appendices ...................................................................... 35
```

**Body Text**

* Font size 12, justified alignment
* Margins: 2.5 cm (top), 3 cm (bottom), 4 cm (left and right)
* Line spacing: 1

Example:
```
The introduction to this thesis provides background information on the topic of artificial intelligence in customer service.

The literature review section discusses the current state of research on AI-powered chatbots in e-commerce.
```

**Headings**

* Font size 14, bold
* Level 1 headings (chapter titles): centered, font size 14, bold
* Level 2 headings (subheadings): left-aligned, font size 12, bold

Example:
```
1. Introduction

1.1 Background Information ............................................... 3
1.2 Research Questions ................................................................... 5
```

**Tables and Figures**

* Font size 10, justified alignment
* Tables: centered, with a caption below the table
* Figures: left-aligned, with a caption above the figure

Example:
```
Table 1: Literature Review Summary ............................................... 10

Figure 1: AI-Powered Chatbot Architecture ...................................... 15
```

**References**

* Font size 12, justified alignment
* Placed at the end of the report
* List all sources cited in the text, in alphabetical order

Example:
```
References

Hafernik, J. E., & Wiant, D. (2012). The impact of artificial intelligence on customer service. Journal of Business Research, 65(1), 47-55.

Larionova, A. (2018). AI-powered chatbots in e-commerce: A review of the literature. International Journal of Electronic Commerce Studies, 9(1), 12-25.
```

**Appendices**

* Font size 10, justified alignment
* Placed at the end of the report
* Numbered in order of reference

Example:
```
Appendix A: Additional Literature Review ............................................... 35

Appendix B: Survey Results ................................................................... 40
```

Please note that this is just an example and you should consult the official Karelia UAS thesis instructions for more detailed guidance on layout, formatting, and content.

This is known as paraphrasing. Paraphrasing involves rephrasing the original text in your own words while maintaining the same meaning. This helps to avoid plagiarism and ensures that you are presenting the information in a way that is clear and concise.

When paraphrasing, it's essential to:

* Use your own language and sentence structure.

Paraphrasing involves rephrasing the original text in your own words while maintaining its meaning and essence. Here are some tips to help you paraphrase effectively:

1.  **Read carefully**: Understand the content, tone, and style of the original text.
2.  **Identify key points**: Determine the main ideas, arguments, or information presented in the text.
3.  **Use your own words**: Express the key points in your own words, avoiding direct quotes or copying.
4.  **Maintain meaning**: Ensure that the paraphrased text conveys the same message and intent as the original.
5.  **Check for clarity**: Verify that the paraphrased text is clear, concise, and easy to understand.

To paraphrase effectively:

*   Use synonyms: Replace words with their equivalents in your own language.
*   Change sentence structure: Rearrange sentences or use different grammatical structures.
*   Add or remove details: Include or exclude specific information to make the text more concise or detailed.
*   Use examples and anecdotes: Illustrate key points with relevant examples or personal experiences.

When paraphrasing, it's essential to avoid:

*   Direct copying: Don't copy entire sentences or paragraphs from the original text.
*   Word-for-word translation: Avoid translating words directly without considering their context and meaning.
*   Over-reliance on synonyms: Use a mix of synonyms and your own words to maintain clarity and authenticity.

By following these guidelines, you can paraphrase effectively and avoid plagiarism.

When writing about information from books in your own words, follow these steps:

1.  **Read the source carefully**: Understand the content and main ideas presented in the book.
2.  **Take notes**: Record key points, concepts, and quotes that are relevant to your thesis. This will help you paraphrase effectively.
3.  **Paraphrase**: Express the ideas in your own words, avoiding direct copying or translation of sentences from the original text. Use synonyms for key terms and rephrase complex sentences into simpler ones.
4.  **Use your own language**: Avoid using phrases like "According to" or "The author states that." Instead, use transitional phrases like "It is argued that," "Research suggests," or "Studies have shown."
5.  **Maintain the original meaning**: Ensure that your paraphrased text conveys the same information and ideas as the original source.
6.  **Check for plagiarism**: Use tools like Turnitin to detect any potential plagiarism.

Here's an example of how to paraphrase a sentence:

Original: "The increasing use of social media has led to a significant rise in cyberbullying among teenagers." (Source: Smith, J., & Johnson, K. (2020). Cyberbullying and Social Media.)

Paraphrased: "Research indicates that the growing popularity of social media platforms has contributed to an alarming increase in online harassment among adolescents."

In this example:

*   The original sentence is direct and concise.
*   The paraphrased sentence uses synonyms ("increasing use" becomes "growing popularity") and rephrases the idea into a more general statement.

By following these steps, you can effectively paraphrase information from books in your own words while maintaining academic integrity.

questions.correct_answer: An in-text citation in Karelia UAS style must include the author's last name, year of publication, and page number(s). Never use p. or pp. before page numbers — (Hafernik & Wiant 2012, 47) is correct, not (Hafernik & Wiant 2012, p. 47). URL addresses are never included in in-text citations. According to the Karelia UAS thesis instructions, the correct format for citing a source within your thesis text is as follows:

**In-text Citations**

Mark every used source with an in-text citation providing: (1) the author's last name, (2) the year of publication, and (3) the page number(s) where the information appears.

Example: (Nokelainen 2016, 5–6) or *According to Nokelainen (2016, 5–6), …*

**Placement of citations:**
- **Information-centred**: place the citation in brackets after the referenced content, at the end of the sentence or paragraph.
- **Author-centred**: begin the sentence with the author's last name; place year and page in brackets immediately after the name if the reference covers one sentence, or at the end if it spans multiple sentences.

**Punctuation:**
- Single sentence citation: full stop follows the closing bracket.
- Multiple sentence citation: full stop at the end of the last sentence, citation added after it with full stop inside the brackets.

questions.topic: What referencing style does Karelia UAS use?
questions.correct_answer: Karelia UAS uses its own institutional referencing style. It is not APA, Harvard, MLA or any other external style. In-text citations include the author's last name, year of publication, and page number(s) without p. before the page number — for example (Nokelainen 2016, 5-6). The reference list at the end follows the format: Author's last name, Initials. Year. Title. Publisher's location: Publisher. Electronic sources include the full URL and access date. Karelia UAS uses its own institutional reporting and referencing style, which is not based on APA, Harvard, MLA, or any other external style.
